content.decision_question: Will redesigning the admission exam to resist coaching durably reduce SES-driven sorting, or will investment simply shift to new coaching strategies targeting the redesigned format, leaving the underlying inequity intact?
content.evidence_boundary: Monitoring coaching-market behavior and outcome composition before and after a redesigned exam could show whether advantage migrates to new instruments, and cross-national comparisons of coaching-resistant exam designs elsewhere could offer indirect evidence. It cannot establish whether it is fair, per se, for any admission system to use scarce selective seats as a rationing device in the first place — a separate value question about the legitimacy of early selection itself.
content.tension: Redesigning the entry exam to be coaching-resistant (T1) directly targets a documented private-tutoring advantage concentrated among university-educated parents, which is a plausible fairness gain. However, evidence from Finland's 'emphasized classes' and Hungary's own selection literature suggests advantage tends to migrate to whatever new screening instrument is introduced rather than disappearing, raising an empirical question about whether this is a durable fix or a temporary displacement of the same underlying sorting dynamic.
content.title: Admission-instrument redesign: fairness through coaching-resistance versus displacement of advantage
